comment_text.sn35.240:1.4: The verb for “drawing in” (samodahati) here is almost identical with that for immersion (samādahati). The turtle “draws in” its five limbs as a meditator draws the mind in from the senses.
comment_text.sn35.240:1.7: The verb abhininnāmeti (“sticks out”) is the same as that used for “extending” the mind to higher knowledges based on samādhi.
comment_text.sn35.240:3.1: The text is notable for the omission of the stock phrase saying the Buddha spoke the verse. Either the verse was added by redactors (lifted from SN 1.17:3.1), or else the stock phrase has been abbreviated away.

root_text.sn35.240:1.1: “Bhūtapubbaṁ, bhikkhave, kummo kacchapo sāyanhasamayaṁ anunadītīre gocarapasuto ahosi.
root_text.sn35.240:1.2: Siṅgālopi kho, bhikkhave, sāyanhasamayaṁ anunadītīre gocarapasuto ahosi.
root_text.sn35.240:1.3: Addasā kho, bhikkhave, kummo kacchapo siṅgālaṁ dūratova gocarapasutaṁ.
root_text.sn35.240:1.4: Disvāna soṇḍipañcamāni aṅgāni sake kapāle samodahitvā appossukko tuṇhībhūto saṅkasāyati.
root_text.sn35.240:1.5: Siṅgālopi kho, bhikkhave, addasa kummaṁ kacchapaṁ dūratova gocarapasutaṁ.
root_text.sn35.240:1.6: Disvāna yena kummo kacchapo tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā kummaṁ kacchapaṁ paccupaṭṭhito ahosi:
root_text.sn35.240:1.7: ‘yadāyaṁ kummo kacchapo soṇḍipañcamānaṁ aṅgānaṁ aññataraṁ vā aññataraṁ vā aṅgaṁ abhininnāmessati, tattheva naṁ gahetvā uddālitvā khādissāmī’ti.
root_text.sn35.240:1.8: Yadā kho, bhikkhave, kummo kacchapo soṇḍipañcamānaṁ aṅgānaṁ aññataraṁ vā aññataraṁ vā aṅgaṁ na abhininnāmi, atha siṅgālo kummamhā nibbijja pakkāmi, otāraṁ alabhamāno.
root_text.sn35.240:2.1: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, tumhepi māro pāpimā satataṁ samitaṁ paccupaṭṭhito:
root_text.sn35.240:2.2: ‘appeva nāmāhaṁ imesaṁ cakkhuto vā otāraṁ labheyyaṁ …pe…
root_text.sn35.240:2.6: Cakkhunā rūpaṁ disvā mā nimittaggāhino ahuvattha, mā anubyañjanaggāhino.
root_text.sn35.240:2.7: Yatvādhikaraṇamenaṁ cakkhundriyaṁ asaṁvutaṁ viharantaṁ abhijjhādomanassā pāpakā akusalā dhammā anvāssaveyyuṁ, tassa saṁvarāya paṭipajjatha, rakkhatha cakkhundriyaṁ, cakkhundriye saṁvaraṁ āpajjatha.
root_text.sn35.240:2.12: manasā dhammaṁ viññāya mā nimittaggāhino ahuvattha, mā anubyañjanaggāhino.
root_text.sn35.240:2.13: Yatvādhikaraṇamenaṁ manindriyaṁ asaṁvutaṁ viharantaṁ abhijjhādomanassā pāpakā akusalā dhammā anvāssaveyyuṁ, tassa saṁvarāya paṭipajjatha, rakkhatha manindriyaṁ, manindriye saṁvaraṁ āpajjatha.
root_text.sn35.240:2.14: Yato tumhe, bhikkhave, indriyesu guttadvārā viharissatha, atha tumhehipi māro pāpimā nibbijja pakkamissati, otāraṁ alabhamāno—

translation_text.sn35.240:1.1: “Once upon a time, mendicants, a tortoise was grazing along the bank of a river in the afternoon.
translation_text.sn35.240:1.2: At the same time, a jackal was also hunting along the river bank.
translation_text.sn35.240:1.3: The tortoise saw the jackal off in the distance hunting,
translation_text.sn35.240:1.4: so it drew its limbs and neck inside its shell, and kept passive and silent.
translation_text.sn35.240:1.5: But the jackal also saw the tortoise off in the distance grazing.
translation_text.sn35.240:1.6: So it went up to the tortoise and waited nearby, thinking,
translation_text.sn35.240:1.7: ‘When that tortoise sticks one or other of its limbs or neck out from its shell, I’ll grab it right there, rip it out, and eat it!’
translation_text.sn35.240:1.8: But when that tortoise didn’t stick one or other of its limbs or neck out from its shell, the jackal left disappointed, since it couldn’t find a vulnerability.
translation_text.sn35.240:2.1: In the same way, Māra the Wicked is always waiting nearby, thinking:
translation_text.sn35.240:2.2: ‘Hopefully I can find a vulnerability in the eye,
translation_text.sn35.240:2.5: That’s why you should live with sense doors guarded.
translation_text.sn35.240:2.6: When you see a sight with your eyes, don’t get caught up in the features and details.
translation_text.sn35.240:2.7: If the faculty of sight were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, practice restraint, protecting the faculty of sight, and achieving its restraint.
translation_text.sn35.240:2.8: When you hear a sound with your ears …
translation_text.sn35.240:2.9: When you smell an odor with your nose …
translation_text.sn35.240:2.10: When you taste a flavor with your tongue …
translation_text.sn35.240:2.11: When you feel a touch with your body …
translation_text.sn35.240:2.12: When you know an idea with your mind, don’t get caught up in the features and details.
translation_text.sn35.240:2.13: If the faculty of mind were left unrestrained, bad unskillful qualities of covetousness and displeasure would become overwhelming. For this reason, practice restraint, protecting the faculty of mind, and achieving its restraint.
translation_text.sn35.240:2.14: When you live with your sense doors guarded, Māra will leave you disappointed, since he can’t find a vulnerability,
translation_text.sn35.240:2.15: just like the jackal left the tortoise.
translation_text.sn35.240:3.1: A mendicant should collect their thoughts
translation_text.sn35.240:3.2: as a tortoise draws its limbs into its shell.
